original_hypothesis.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation Owner Outside Governance — Deployer EOA Retains Permanent Control
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The deployment script (DeployOnL1.s.sol) deploys AutomataDcapV3Attestation with `new` (not proxied), and the constructor sets owner to msg.sender (the deployer EOA). The contract has no ownership transfer function. All other protocol contracts transfer ownership to the timelock governance system. The attestation contract — which controls all SGX attestation configuration — remains under the deployer's direct EOA control, outside the governance/timelock system. A compromised deployer key grants permanent, irrevocable control over attestation trust configuration.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete deployment gap where the attestation contract's admin is decoupled from the rest of the protocol's governance. The deployer EOA has powers equivalent to the timelock but without governance oversight.
deployment_assumptions: Deployer EOA retains permanent ownership of attestation contract
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol: `AutomataDcapV3Attestation automateDcapV3Attestation = new AutomataDcapV3Attestation(address(sigVerifyLib), address(pemCertChainLib));` — no transferOwnership call; contract has no such function

proof_of_signal.pattern: owner = msg.sender in constructor; no transferOwnership function exists; deployment script never transfers ownership to timelock

preconditions: Deployer EOA private key is compromised
Attacker uses the key to reconfigure attestation trust

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All other contracts transfer ownership to timelock; this one does not and cannot
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Single EOA with no governance oversight

operational_questions: Is the deployer EOA key secured in hardware/multisig?
Has the team considered the risk of this key being separate from governance?
Would adding a malicious MrEnclave immediately allow forged proofs?
validation_requirements: Verify the deployer address on mainnet and its security posture
Assess impact of malicious configureTcbInfoJson or setMrEnclave calls

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise the deployer EOA private key (phishing, key extraction, insider)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call setMrEnclave(attacker_enclave, true) to whitelist malicious enclave
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call configureTcbInfoJson with permissive TCB levels
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Submit forged SGX attestations that pass verification

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete control over SGX attestation trust model
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Ability to forge SGX proofs, potentially compromising rollup security
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Must redeploy attestation contract and update AddressManager via timelock governance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpected setMrEnclave/configureTcbInfoJson calls from deployer EOA
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: New trusted enclaves or TCB configurations appearing without governance proposal
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Admin calls outside normal operational schedule

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
